categories.label: I built a website and want to share it

vehicles.name: GitHub Pages
vehicles.description: Free hosting tied to a GitHub repository. Push your files, get a URL. If your project is already on GitHub, this is the simplest way to put it on the internet.
vehicles.costDetail: Free for public and private repos
vehicles.bestFor: Simple sites, tools with no backend, portfolios, documentation

example.name: Ask Bigger
example.note: An educational site deployed entirely on GitHub Pages — no build step, no framework

vehicles.name: Cloudflare Pages
vehicles.description: Free static hosting from Cloudflare with optional server-side functions. Similar to GitHub Pages but with some extra capabilities if you need a tiny bit of backend logic.
vehicles.bestFor: Static sites that need a bit of server logic, fast global delivery
instructions: Create a free Cloudflare account at cloudflare.com
Go to Workers & Pages and click 'Create application'
Connect your GitHub repo (or drag and drop your files)
Cloudflare auto-detects your framework and builds it
Your site goes live at a .pages.dev URL
Optionally connect a custom domain

vehicles.name: Netlify
vehicles.description: Free hosting that also handles forms, identity (login), and serverless functions. Good if your site needs to collect form submissions or run small bits of server code.
vehicles.costDetail: Free tier includes 100GB bandwidth, 300 build minutes/month
vehicles.bestFor: Sites that need form handling, A/B testing, or light serverless functions
instructions: Create a free account at netlify.com
Click 'Add new site' and connect your GitHub repo
Netlify auto-detects your build settings
Your site deploys to a .netlify.app URL
For forms: add a 'netlify' attribute to any HTML form — submissions go to your Netlify dashboard
For serverless functions: add a netlify/functions folder with your function files

vehicles.name: Vercel
vehicles.description: Free hosting that works especially well with React and Next.js. Includes serverless functions so you can add backend logic without managing a server.
vehicles.bestFor: React/Next.js apps, sites needing an API, anything beyond pure static files
instructions: Create a free account at vercel.com
Click 'Add New Project' and import your GitHub repo
Vercel auto-detects your framework (React, Next.js, etc.)
Set any environment variables your project needs
Click Deploy — your site goes live at a .vercel.app URL
Every git push automatically redeploys

vehicles.name: Surge.sh
vehicles.description: The fastest way to publish a static site. One command in the terminal and your site is live. No GitHub needed, no configuration files.
vehicles.costDetail: Free for basic use, paid tier for custom domains and SSL
vehicles.bestFor: Quick demos, getting something online in 30 seconds
instructions: Install surge: npm install -g surge
Navigate to your project folder in the terminal
Run: surge
Enter your email and a password (first time only)
Choose a subdomain or accept the random one
Your site is live — that's it

vehicles.name: Neocities
vehicles.description: Free web hosting with a retro, indie ethos. Upload HTML/CSS/JS files and get a site. Good vibes, simple interface, no corporate overhead.
vehicles.costDetail: Free with 1GB storage, paid supporter tier for more
vehicles.bestFor: Personal sites, creative projects, learning HTML
instructions: Create a free account at neocities.org
Upload your HTML, CSS, and JS files through the web dashboard
Your site is live at yourname.neocities.org
Edit files directly in the browser if you want
Optional: use the Neocities CLI for command-line uploads

vehicles.name: Glitch
vehicles.description: Code in your browser and deploy instantly. Glitch gives you an online editor and free hosting in one place. Great for quick experiments you want to share immediately.
vehicles.costDetail: Free apps sleep after 5 min inactivity, wake on request
vehicles.bestFor: Quick demos, prototypes, collaborative hacking, teaching
instructions: Go to glitch.com and click 'New Project'
Choose a starter template or import from GitHub
Edit code in the browser — changes go live instantly
Your app runs at yourproject.glitch.me
Share the URL or let others 'remix' (fork) your project
Note: free apps sleep after 5 minutes of inactivity

vehicles.name: Replit
vehicles.description: Online coding environment that can build and deploy in almost any programming language. Code in your browser, click deploy, done. Also has AI coding assistants built in.
vehicles.costDetail: Free for public projects, paid for always-on and private
vehicles.bestFor: Prototyping, sharing runnable projects, multi-language projects
instructions: Create a free account at replit.com
Click 'Create Repl' and choose your language
Paste or write your code in the browser editor
Click the Run button to test
Click Deploy to get a public URL
Share the link — others can also fork your Repl

categories.label: I need a real backend / database

vehicles.name: Vercel + Neon
vehicles.description: A popular free combo: Vercel hosts your app and Neon gives you a real PostgreSQL database in the cloud. This is what you use when your tool needs to remember things — user accounts, saved data, anything that should survive closing the browser.
vehicles.costDetail: Both have generous free tiers — Neon gives 0.5GB storage free
vehicles.bestFor: Full-stack apps with persistent data, user accounts, anything beyond a static site

example.name: Hockey Tracker Pro
example.note: Started as a static site, then added a real database when coaches needed accounts and season-long stats

vehicles.name: Vercel + Supabase
vehicles.description: Like Vercel + Neon but Supabase gives you more out of the box: a database, user login/signup, file storage, and realtime features. Think of it as a backend-in-a-box.
vehicles.costDetail: Free tier: 500MB database, 1GB file storage, 50K monthly active users
vehicles.bestFor: Apps needing user auth, file uploads, realtime updates, or all three

vehicles.name: Railway
vehicles.description: One-click deploy from GitHub. Railway detects what you built, sets up the right environment, and deploys it. Also offers databases (Postgres, MySQL, Redis) with one click.
vehicles.costDetail: Free trial with $5 credit, then pay-as-you-go
vehicles.bestFor: Quick full-stack deploys when you want minimal configuration
instructions: Create a free account at railway.app
Click 'New Project' and connect your GitHub repo
Railway auto-detects your app type and deploys
To add a database: click 'New' → 'Database' → choose Postgres/MySQL/Redis
Railway gives you a connection string — add it as an environment variable
Your app gets a .railway.app URL

vehicles.name: Render
vehicles.description: Managed hosting for web apps, APIs, and databases. Your app stays running all the time (unlike some free tiers that sleep). Good when you need something more reliable than a hobby tier.
vehicles.costDetail: Free tier for static sites and web services (with spin-down), paid for always-on
vehicles.bestFor: Apps that need to be always-on, background jobs, cron tasks
instructions: Create a free account at render.com
Click 'New' and choose Web Service, Static Site, or Background Worker
Connect your GitHub repo
Set build and start commands
For a database: create a new PostgreSQL instance from the dashboard
Your service gets a .onrender.com URL

vehicles.name: Fly.io
vehicles.description: Deploy apps to servers around the world so they load fast no matter where your users are. Uses Docker containers, which means it can run almost anything.
vehicles.bestFor: Full-stack apps that need to be fast globally, anything Docker-based
instructions: Install the Fly CLI: curl -L https://fly.io/install.sh | sh
Run: fly auth signup (or fly auth login)
In your project folder, run: fly launch
Follow the prompts to choose a region and app name
Run: fly deploy
Your app goes live at yourapp.fly.dev

vehicles.name: Deno Deploy
vehicles.description: Run JavaScript or TypeScript code on servers around the world with zero configuration. Starts instantly, scales automatically. Good for APIs and bots.
vehicles.bestFor: APIs, bots, lightweight server-side logic
instructions: Create a free account at dash.deno.com
Click 'New Project' and connect your GitHub repo
Choose your entry point file (e.g., main.ts)
Deploy — your app runs at yourproject.deno.dev
Every push to your repo triggers a new deployment
Supports TypeScript and npm packages natively

vehicles.name: Cloudflare Workers
vehicles.description: Run small pieces of JavaScript at the edge of the internet — meaning your code runs on servers close to whoever is using it. Extremely fast and extremely cheap.
vehicles.bestFor: APIs, URL shorteners, redirects, lightweight server logic
instructions: Install Wrangler: npm install -g wrangler
Run: wrangler login
Run: wrangler init my-project
Write your worker code in src/index.ts
Test locally: wrangler dev
Deploy: wrangler deploy
Your worker runs at yourname.workers.dev

vehicles.name: Val Town
vehicles.description: Write a function, get a URL. Val Town lets you write small scripts that run in the cloud and can be called as API endpoints. No deployment step — just write and it is live.
vehicles.costDetail: Free tier with limits on compute and storage
vehicles.bestFor: Quick API endpoints, webhooks, scheduled scripts, prototyping
instructions: Sign up at val.town
Click 'New Val' and write your function
Your function instantly gets a URL endpoint
Call it from anywhere: fetch('https://yourname-yourval.web.val.run')
No deploy step — save and it's live
Add environment variables in Settings for API keys

vehicles.name: PocketBase
vehicles.description: A complete backend in a single file. Gives you a database (SQLite), user authentication, file storage, and an admin panel. Download one file, run it, and you have a backend.
vehicles.bestFor: Adding a backend to a frontend project without cloud services
instructions: Download the PocketBase binary from pocketbase.io
Run: ./pocketbase serve
Open the admin UI at localhost:8090/_/
Create your collections (tables) using the visual editor
PocketBase auto-generates a REST API for your data
Connect your frontend using the PocketBase JavaScript SDK
For production: deploy the binary to Fly.io or any VPS

vehicles.name: Firebase
vehicles.description: Google's all-in-one app platform. Database that syncs in realtime, user login, file storage, hosting, push notifications. Lots of features, but also lots of Google lock-in.
vehicles.costDetail: Spark plan (free) covers most hobby projects
vehicles.bestFor: Apps needing realtime data sync, push notifications, quick prototyping
instructions: Create a project at console.firebase.google.com
Install Firebase CLI: npm install -g firebase-tools
Run: firebase init (select the services you need)
For database: choose Firestore and create collections in the console
For auth: enable sign-in methods in Authentication settings
For hosting: run firebase deploy
Your site goes live at yourproject.web.app

vehicles.name: Supabase
vehicles.description: An open-source alternative to Firebase built on PostgreSQL. Database, authentication, file storage, edge functions, and realtime — all with a SQL database you can query directly.
vehicles.bestFor: Full backend without vendor lock-in, teams that want SQL
instructions: Create a free project at supabase.com
Use the Table Editor to create your database tables
Go to Authentication → enable your preferred sign-in methods
Copy your project URL and anon key from Settings → API
Install the client: npm install @supabase/supabase-js
Connect from your frontend using the URL and key
Row Level Security policies control who can see what data

vehicles.name: Turso
vehicles.description: SQLite databases that run on the edge — meaning your database is distributed globally and responds fast everywhere. Uses libSQL, an open fork of SQLite.
vehicles.bestFor: Apps that need a SQL database but not a heavyweight cloud Postgres
instructions: Install the Turso CLI: brew install tursodatabase/tap/turso
Run: turso auth signup
Create a database: turso db create my-database
Get your connection URL: turso db show my-database --url
Create an auth token: turso db tokens create my-database
Use the @libsql/client npm package to connect from your app
Data replicates globally for fast reads everywhere

vehicles.name: Upstash
vehicles.description: Serverless Redis (a fast key-value store) and message queues in the cloud. Pay only for what you use. Good for caching, rate limiting, and background job processing.
vehicles.costDetail: Free: 10K commands/day for Redis, limited QStash messages
vehicles.bestFor: Caching, rate limiting, session storage, background jobs
instructions: Create a free account at upstash.com
Create a new Redis database from the console
Copy the REST URL and token
Install: npm install @upstash/redis
Use the REST client to get/set data from your app
For scheduled jobs: create a QStash topic and publish messages

vehicles.name: Electron
vehicles.description: Take your web app and wrap it in a desktop application. The result is a .app (Mac) or .exe (Windows) that people download and run like any other program. Slack, VS Code, and Discord are all Electron apps.
vehicles.bestFor: Cross-platform desktop apps, turning existing web apps into desktop apps
instructions: Make sure Node.js is installed
Create a new folder and run: npm init -y
Install Electron: npm install electron --save-dev
Create main.js (loads your web app in a window) and index.html
Add a start script: "electron ."
Run: npm start to test locally
Install electron-builder to create distributable .dmg/.exe files
Run the build command to package your app

vehicles.name: Tauri
vehicles.description: Like Electron but much smaller and faster. Uses your operating system's built-in browser engine instead of bundling Chrome. The resulting app is a fraction of the size.
vehicles.bestFor: Lightweight desktop apps, performance-sensitive tools
instructions: Install Rust from rustup.rs
Install the Tauri CLI: cargo install tauri-cli
In your web project, run: cargo tauri init
Answer the prompts about your project
Run: cargo tauri dev (to test locally)
Run: cargo tauri build (to create a distributable app)
Output: .dmg for Mac, .msi for Windows, .deb/.AppImage for Linux

vehicles.name: macOS Menubar App (SwiftUI)
vehicles.description: A tiny Mac app that lives in your menu bar — the row of icons at the top-right of your screen. Click the icon, get a dropdown with useful info or quick actions. Feels native because it is native.
vehicles.bestFor: Quick-access Mac utilities, status indicators, ambient info displays
instructions: Open Xcode (free from the Mac App Store)
Create a new macOS App project
Set the app to be an 'Agent' application (no dock icon)
Create an NSStatusItem for the menu bar icon
Build your dropdown view in SwiftUI
Run and test — the icon appears in your menu bar
To share: export an .app bundle from Xcode

vehicles.name: macOS Menubar App (Python/rumps)
vehicles.description: Same idea as a SwiftUI menubar app but built with Python using the rumps library. Simpler to set up if you are already working in Python. No Xcode needed.
vehicles.bestFor: Mac menubar tools when Python is already your language
instructions: Install Python 3 if not already installed
Install rumps: pip install rumps
Create a Python file with your menu bar app logic
Use @rumps.clicked decorators for menu item actions
Run your script — the icon appears in the menu bar
Optional: use py2app to package as a standalone .app

vehicles.name: Wails
vehicles.description: Build a desktop app with a Go backend and a web frontend. Combines the performance of Go with the flexibility of HTML/CSS/JS for the interface.
vehicles.bestFor: Desktop apps that need strong backend performance
instructions: Install Go from go.dev
Install Wails: go install github.com/wailsapp/wails/v2/cmd/wails@latest
Create a new project: wails init -n myapp -t svelte (or react/vue)
Build your frontend in the frontend/ folder
Write Go backend code in app.go
Run: wails dev (for development)
Run: wails build (to create a distributable app)

vehicles.name: PyInstaller
vehicles.description: Package a Python script into a standalone executable that anyone can run without installing Python. Double-click to run. Works on Mac, Windows, and Linux.
vehicles.bestFor: Distributing Python tools to people who don't have Python installed
instructions: Install PyInstaller: pip install pyinstaller
Navigate to your project folder
Run: pyinstaller --onefile your_script.py
Find your executable in the dist/ folder
Test it by running the executable directly
Share the executable — no Python installation needed to run it
For a Mac .app bundle: add the --windowed flag

vehicles.name: pkg (Node.js)
vehicles.description: Package a Node.js script into a single executable file. The person running it doesn't need Node.js installed — just download and run.
vehicles.bestFor: Distributing Node.js CLI tools without requiring Node installation
instructions: Install pkg: npm install -g pkg
Make sure your package.json has a 'bin' field pointing to your main script
Run: pkg . (builds for all platforms)
Or target specific platforms: pkg . --targets node18-macos-x64
Find executables in the output folder
Share — recipients don't need Node.js installed

vehicles.name: AppleScript App
vehicles.description: Write an AppleScript (Mac's built-in automation language) and save it as a .app file. Double-click to run. Good for simple Mac automations that need to feel like real apps.
vehicles.bestFor: Simple Mac automations packaged as clickable apps
instructions: Open Script Editor (built into Mac, in Applications → Utilities)
Write your AppleScript
Go to File → Export
Change File Format to 'Application'
Save it wherever you want
Double-click the .app file to run it
Drag it to Applications folder if you want it permanently

vehicles.name: Automator App
vehicles.description: Mac's built-in workflow builder, saved as a standalone application. Drag and drop actions like "rename files" or "convert images" and save the whole thing as an app anyone can double-click.
vehicles.bestFor: File processing, batch operations, system tasks on Mac
instructions: Open Automator (built into Mac, in Applications)
Choose 'Application' as the document type
Drag actions from the library into your workflow
Common actions: Run Shell Script, Copy Files, Rename Finder Items
Click Run to test
Save — it becomes a .app you can double-click
Drag it to your dock or Applications folder

vehicles.name: macOS Screensaver
vehicles.description: Build a custom screensaver that runs when your Mac is idle. Could show data visualizations, generative art, a photo slideshow, or anything you can display on screen.
vehicles.bestFor: Ambient data displays, generative art, creative installations
instructions: Open Xcode and create a new project
Choose the Screen Saver template
Implement your drawing code in the ScreenSaverView subclass
Build the project — it outputs a .saver file
Double-click the .saver file to install it
Go to System Settings → Screen Saver to select it
Share the .saver file for others to install

vehicles.name: macOS Widget
vehicles.description: A small widget that appears in your Mac's Notification Center or on your desktop. Glanceable info like weather, stats, countdowns, or quick actions.
vehicles.bestFor: At-a-glance information on your Mac desktop
instructions: Open Xcode and create a new project (or add a Widget Extension to existing)
Choose the Widget Extension template
Define your timeline entries (what data to show and when to refresh)
Build the widget view in SwiftUI
Run on your Mac to test
Widget appears in Notification Center and on the desktop

vehicles.name: Windows System Tray App
vehicles.description: An app that lives in the Windows notification area (bottom-right of the screen). Similar concept to a Mac menubar app — small, always accessible, out of the way.
vehicles.costDetail: Can be built with .NET, Electron, or Python
vehicles.bestFor: Quick-access Windows utilities, status monitors
instructions: Choose your framework: .NET (WinForms), Electron, or Python (pystray)
Create a system tray icon using NotifyIcon (.NET) or equivalent
Add a context menu with your actions
Handle click events for each menu item
Set the app to run at startup if desired (add to registry or Startup folder)
Package as an installer using NSIS, Inno Setup, or similar

categories.label: I want it on my phone

vehicles.name: Progressive Web App (PWA)
vehicles.description: Turn your website into something that feels like a phone app. Users can "install" it from the browser — it gets an icon on the home screen, works offline, and runs without the browser toolbar. No app store needed.
vehicles.costDetail: Just needs proper configuration of your existing site
vehicles.bestFor: App-like experience without app store approval, offline-capable tools
instructions: Add a manifest.json to your project with app name, icons, and display settings
Create a service worker file for offline caching
Add <link rel='manifest' href='manifest.json'> to your HTML
Add iOS meta tags: apple-mobile-web-app-capable, status-bar-style
Generate app icons in required sizes (192x192, 512x512 minimum)
Deploy to any HTTPS host
Test: open on your phone and look for the 'Add to Home Screen' option

vehicles.name: Expo / React Native
vehicles.description: Build real native mobile apps using JavaScript. The apps look and feel native because they use actual iOS and Android components. Expo simplifies the process significantly compared to raw React Native.
vehicles.costDetail: Free to build, app store fees to publish ($99/year Apple, $25 one-time Google)
vehicles.bestFor: Real native mobile apps that need device features (camera, GPS, etc.)
instructions: Install Node.js and the Expo CLI: npx create-expo-app my-app
Navigate into the folder and run: npx expo start
Scan the QR code with your phone to preview (install Expo Go app first)
Build your screens using React Native components
For iOS builds: run npx expo build:ios (needs Apple Developer account)
For Android: run npx expo build:android
Submit to App Store / Google Play from the Expo dashboard

vehicles.name: Capacitor
vehicles.description: Take an existing web app and wrap it in a native mobile shell. The result goes on the App Store or Google Play. You keep your web codebase and add native features as needed.
vehicles.costDetail: Open source, app store fees to publish
vehicles.bestFor: Converting existing web apps into mobile apps without rewriting
instructions: Install Capacitor in your web project: npm install @capacitor/core @capacitor/cli
Initialize: npx cap init
Build your web app: npm run build
Add platforms: npx cap add ios && npx cap add android
Sync your built files: npx cap sync
Open in Xcode: npx cap open ios (or Android Studio: npx cap open android)
Build and run on your device from the native IDE

vehicles.name: TestFlight
vehicles.description: Apple's official way to share iPhone and iPad apps with testers before publishing to the App Store. Send people a link, they install your app. Up to 10,000 testers, no App Store review needed.
vehicles.costDetail: Free but requires Apple Developer account ($99/year)
vehicles.bestFor: Sharing iOS apps with a small group before full release
instructions: Build your iOS app in Xcode
Create an App Store Connect record at appstoreconnect.apple.com
Archive your app in Xcode (Product → Archive)
Upload the archive to App Store Connect
Add testers by email (up to 10,000 external testers)
Testers get a link to install your app via the TestFlight app
No App Store review needed for internal testers

vehicles.name: Shortcuts (iOS/macOS)
vehicles.description: Apple's visual automation tool built into every iPhone, iPad, and Mac. Chain together actions (send a message, create a file, call an API) into one-tap or voice-activated workflows. No coding, visual drag-and-drop.
vehicles.bestFor: Quick personal automations, voice-activated tools ("Hey Siri, run my tool")
instructions: Open the Shortcuts app on your iPhone, iPad, or Mac
Tap the + button to create a new shortcut
Search for and add actions (e.g., Get Contents of URL, Show Result)
Chain actions together — output of one feeds into the next
Give it a name and icon
Add to Home Screen for one-tap access
Say 'Hey Siri, [shortcut name]' to run it by voice

vehicles.name: Scriptable
vehicles.description: Write JavaScript that runs on your iPhone with access to native features — create home screen widgets, interact with your calendar, send notifications, process files. A programmable layer on top of your phone.
vehicles.costDetail: Free app on the App Store
vehicles.bestFor: iPhone home screen widgets, personal automations, quick tools
instructions: Install Scriptable from the App Store (free)
Open the app and tap + to create a new script
Write JavaScript using Scriptable's APIs (web requests, notifications, etc.)
For a home screen widget: implement the widget API and add as iOS widget
Share scripts via iCloud links
Scripts can also be triggered from Shortcuts app

vehicles.name: GitHub Actions
vehicles.description: Automated tasks that run on GitHub's servers, triggered by events (code push, schedule, manual button). Runs for free on public repos. Common uses: build your project, run tests, deploy, or run a script on a schedule.
vehicles.costDetail: Free for public repos, 2,000 minutes/month for private
vehicles.bestFor: Scheduled tasks, CI/CD, repo automation, anything triggered by code changes

vehicles.name: Agentic Workflows
vehicles.description: GitHub Actions powered by AI. Instead of writing step-by-step scripts, you describe what you want in natural language and an AI agent figures out how to do it. Runs in GitHub Actions with Copilot.
vehicles.bestFor: AI-powered repo automation, complex tasks described in natural language
instructions: Create a .md file in your .github/workflows/ directory
Add frontmatter with name, description, on (trigger), and permissions
Write natural language instructions for what the agent should do
The agent reads your repo context and acts accordingly
Triggered by events you specify (issue opened, PR created, schedule, etc.)
Check the Actions tab to see runs and results

vehicles.name: Cron Jobs (Cloud)
vehicles.description: Run a script on a schedule in the cloud — every hour, every day, every Monday at 9am. Services like Railway, Render, and Fly.io let you set this up with their free tiers.
vehicles.bestFor: Recurring tasks, daily reports, data syncing, monitoring
instructions: Choose a hosting provider with cron support (Railway, Render, Fly.io)
Create your script that does the recurring work
Deploy the script to your chosen provider
Configure the cron schedule in the provider's dashboard or config file
Common format: '0 9 * * *' means daily at 9am UTC
Check logs in the provider's dashboard to verify it's running

vehicles.name: macOS LaunchAgent
vehicles.description: A background process that runs on your Mac — at login, on a schedule, or when certain conditions are met. Built into macOS. Good for local automations that don't need the cloud.
vehicles.bestFor: Local Mac automations, background daemons, scheduled local tasks
instructions: Create a .plist file describing your scheduled task
Save it in ~/Library/LaunchAgents/
Key fields: Label, ProgramArguments, StartInterval or StartCalendarInterval
Load it: launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/your-agent.plist
Check it's running: launchctl list | grep your-label
View logs in Console.app or redirect stdout/stderr in the plist
To stop: launchctl unload the plist file

vehicles.name: Zapier
vehicles.description: Connect apps together with if-this-then-that logic. No coding required. "When a form is submitted, add a row to Google Sheets and send a Slack message." Thousands of apps supported.
vehicles.bestFor: Connecting apps without code, simple multi-step automations
instructions: Create a free account at zapier.com
Click 'Create Zap'
Choose your trigger app and event (e.g., 'New Form Submission in Google Forms')
Connect your account for that app
Choose your action app and event (e.g., 'Send Message in Slack')
Map the data fields from trigger to action
Test the Zap, then turn it on

vehicles.name: Make (Integromat)
vehicles.description: Visual automation builder — more powerful than Zapier for complex workflows. Drag and drop modules on a canvas, connect them, add branching logic. Free tier is more generous than Zapier's.
vehicles.bestFor: Complex multi-step automations with branching, data transformation
instructions: Create a free account at make.com
Click 'Create a new scenario'
Add modules by clicking the + icon on the canvas
Connect your apps and configure each module
Draw connections between modules to define the flow
Use filters and routers for conditional logic
Run once to test, then activate the scenario

vehicles.name: n8n
vehicles.description: Self-hosted workflow automation. Like Zapier/Make but you run it on your own server, so there are no limits on usage. Open source and free. Also available as a cloud service.
vehicles.costDetail: Free to self-host, cloud version has paid plans
vehicles.bestFor: Full control over automations, no vendor limits, privacy-sensitive workflows
instructions: Install n8n: npm install -g n8n (or use Docker)
Run: n8n start
Open the editor at localhost:5678
Add nodes by clicking + and searching for your apps
Connect nodes to define the workflow
Click 'Execute Workflow' to test
Activate the workflow to run it automatically

vehicles.name: IFTTT
vehicles.description: The simplest automation tool: if this happens, then do that. One trigger, one action. Great for smart home stuff and simple connections between services.
vehicles.bestFor: Smart home automation, simple single-step triggers
instructions: Create a free account at ifttt.com
Click 'Create' to make a new Applet
Click 'If This' and choose your trigger service and event
Click 'Then That' and choose your action service and event
Configure the action details
Click 'Continue' and 'Finish'
The Applet runs automatically when triggered

vehicles.name: Windows Task Scheduler
vehicles.description: Built into every Windows PC. Run a script or program on a schedule or when you log in. The Windows equivalent of macOS LaunchAgents.
vehicles.bestFor: Scheduled Windows automations, background tasks on your PC
instructions: Open Task Scheduler from the Start menu
Click 'Create Basic Task'
Give it a name and description
Choose the trigger: daily, weekly, at startup, etc.
Choose 'Start a program' as the action
Browse to your script or executable
Click Finish — the task will run on schedule

vehicles.name: Val Town (Scheduled)
vehicles.description: Write a script in Val Town and set it to run on a schedule. No server to manage — just write the code and set the interval. Good for lightweight recurring tasks.
vehicles.costDetail: Free tier with limited schedule frequency
vehicles.bestFor: Lightweight scheduled jobs, monitoring, data collection
instructions: Sign up at val.town
Create a new Val
Write your function
Click the schedule icon (clock) next to the Val name

vehicles.bestFor: Notifications, two-way interactions via text, reaching non-tech users
instructions: Create a free Twilio account at twilio.com
Get a Twilio phone number (free trial includes one)
Install the Twilio SDK: npm install twilio
To send SMS: use client.messages.create() with your Twilio number
To receive SMS: set up a webhook URL in the Twilio console
Deploy your webhook handler (Vercel, Railway, etc.)
Test by texting your Twilio number

categories.label: I want to distribute a CLI tool

vehicles.name: NPM Package
vehicles.description: Publish your tool on NPM (the JavaScript package registry). Anyone can install it with one command: npm install -g your-tool. If it is a CLI tool, they type the command name and it runs.
vehicles.bestFor: JavaScript/Node.js CLI tools, globally installable commands

example.name: CLI Noob Mode
example.note: A Copilot CLI translation layer published as an NPM package

instructions: Make sure your package.json has: name, version, description, and bin field
The bin field maps your command name to your script: {"mytool": "./index.js"}
Add #!/usr/bin/env node at the top of your entry script
Create an account at npmjs.com
Run: npm login
Run: npm publish
Test: npm install -g your-package-name && your-command

vehicles.name: PyPI Package
vehicles.description: Publish your Python tool on PyPI (the Python Package Index). Anyone can install it with: pip install your-tool. The Python equivalent of NPM.
vehicles.bestFor: Python CLI tools, pip-installable libraries
instructions: Create a pyproject.toml with project name, version, and dependencies
Add a [project.scripts] section for CLI entry points
Install build tools: pip install build twine
Build: python -m build (creates dist/ folder)
Create an account at pypi.org
Upload: twine upload dist/*
Test: pip install your-package-name

vehicles.name: Homebrew Formula
vehicles.description: Add your tool to Homebrew, the Mac (and Linux) package manager. Users install with: brew install your-tool. The canonical way to distribute command-line tools on Mac.
vehicles.costDetail: Free, submit to homebrew-core or create a tap
vehicles.bestFor: Mac command-line tools, one-command installation
instructions: Create a GitHub repo for your tool with a tagged release
Create a separate 'homebrew-tap' repo (e.g., dvelton/homebrew-mytool)
Write a Formula (Ruby file) that specifies: URL, SHA256, and install steps
Push the formula to your tap repo
Users install with: brew tap yourname/mytool && brew install mytool
For wider distribution: submit a PR to homebrew/homebrew-core

vehicles.name: GitHub Releases
vehicles.description: Attach compiled binaries (executables) to a GitHub release. Users go to your repo, click Releases, download the right file for their OS, and run it. Simple, no package manager needed.
vehicles.bestFor: Distributing standalone executables, cross-platform binaries
instructions: Tag your code: git tag v1.0.0 && git push --tags
Go to your repo on GitHub → Releases → 'Create a new release'
Select your tag and write release notes
Drag and drop your compiled binaries (one per OS/architecture)
Publish the release
Users download the right binary for their system
Automate with GitHub Actions: build binaries on tag push and attach to release

vehicles.name: Go Binary
vehicles.description: Compile a Go program into a single file with zero dependencies. The resulting binary works on any matching OS without installing Go or any runtime. Cross-compile for Mac, Windows, and Linux from any platform.
vehicles.costDetail: Go is free and open source
vehicles.bestFor: Cross-platform CLI tools that need to "just work" with no dependencies
instructions: Write your tool in Go (go.dev to get started)
Build for your current OS: go build -o mytool
Cross-compile: GOOS=windows GOARCH=amd64 go build -o mytool.exe
Common targets: darwin/amd64 (Mac Intel), darwin/arm64 (Mac M-series), linux/amd64, windows/amd64
Each output is a single executable with zero dependencies
Distribute via GitHub Releases or Homebrew

vehicles.name: Shell Script (curl | bash)
vehicles.description: Host a shell script on GitHub and let people install with one command: curl -sSL your-url | bash. Common for developer tools. Quick and dirty but effective for getting something installed fast.
vehicles.costDetail: Free, just a file on GitHub
vehicles.bestFor: Quick installation scripts, developer tools, setup automations
instructions: Write your install script (bash or sh)
Host it in a GitHub repo (e.g., install.sh at the root)
Get the raw URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/you/repo/main/install.sh
Users install with: curl -sSL your-raw-url | bash
Make sure the script is idempotent (safe to run twice)
Add error handling and clear output messages

vehicles.name: Docker Image
vehicles.description: Package your tool in a Docker container — a self-contained environment with everything it needs to run. Users install Docker, then run one command to start your tool. Guarantees it works the same everywhere.
vehicles.costDetail: Free to build and publish on Docker Hub
vehicles.bestFor: Tools with complex dependencies, consistent environments, server applications
instructions: Create a Dockerfile in your project root
Define the base image, copy your files, install dependencies, set the start command
Build: docker build -t yourusername/mytool .
Test locally: docker run yourusername/mytool
Create an account at hub.docker.com
Push: docker push yourusername/mytool
Users run: docker run yourusername/mytool

categories.label: I want to share data or content

vehicles.name: RSS Feed
vehicles.description: An auto-updating content feed that anyone can subscribe to using a feed reader. When you publish something new, subscribers get it automatically. Old technology that still works great.
vehicles.costDetail: Just an XML file on any web server
vehicles.bestFor: Publishing updates, blog feeds, content that people should follow
instructions: Create an XML file following the RSS 2.0 format
Include channel info: title, link, description
Add item elements for each piece of content
Host the XML file on GitHub Pages or any web server
Add a <link> tag to your HTML pointing to the feed
Automate updates with a script or GitHub Action
Test with a feed reader like Feedly or NetNewsWire

vehicles.name: GitHub README Dashboard
vehicles.description: A README.md that auto-updates itself using GitHub Actions. The README becomes a live dashboard showing stats, status, or data that refreshes on a schedule. Your repo page becomes a display.
vehicles.bestFor: Live stats, status pages, automated data displays
instructions: Create a special repo named after your GitHub username (e.g., dvelton/dvelton)
Add a README.md — this appears on your GitHub profile
Create a GitHub Action that runs on a schedule
The Action runs a script that generates/updates the README with fresh data
Commit the updated README back to the repo
Your GitHub profile now shows live, auto-updating content

vehicles.name: GitHub Gist
vehicles.description: A shareable code snippet or small file hosted on GitHub. Create a gist, get a URL. Can contain multiple files, supports versioning, and can be embedded in other pages. Good for small, sharable tools.
vehicles.bestFor: Sharing scripts, configs, snippets, small standalone tools
instructions: Go to gist.github.com
Paste your code or content
Give it a filename (the extension determines syntax highlighting)
Choose Public or Secret
Click 'Create Gist'
Share the URL — anyone can view, fork, or embed it
Edit anytime — gists support full version history

vehicles.name: JSON API on GitHub Pages
vehicles.description: Serve static JSON files from GitHub Pages as a read-only API. No server, no database — just JSON files that anyone can fetch. Update the data by pushing to the repo.
vehicles.bestFor: Simple read-only APIs, reference data, configuration endpoints
instructions: Create JSON files with your data (e.g., data/items.json)
Commit them to a GitHub repo with Pages enabled
Your JSON is now accessible at: https://yourusername.github.io/repo/data/items.json
Fetch it from any frontend: fetch('https://yourusername.github.io/repo/data/items.json')
Update data by committing new JSON files
Add CORS headers if needed (GitHub Pages allows cross-origin by default)

vehicles.name: Datasette
vehicles.description: Drop a SQLite database file in, get an instant web interface with search, filtering, JSON API, and data visualization. Turns any dataset into an explorable, queryable website.
vehicles.costDetail: Open source, deploy on free tiers
vehicles.bestFor: Making datasets explorable, data journalism, public data tools
instructions: Install Datasette: pip install datasette
Run: datasette your-data.db (point it at any SQLite database)
Browse your data at localhost:8001
Every table gets a web interface with search, filter, and sort
JSON API is automatic: add .json to any URL
Deploy to Fly.io, Google Cloud Run, or Vercel for public access
Use csvs-to-sqlite to convert CSV files into a queryable database

vehicles.name: Observable Notebook
vehicles.description: An interactive data notebook on the web. Write analysis code, create visualizations, and publish it all as a live, interactive document that anyone can view, fork, and play with.
vehicles.bestFor: Data visualization, shareable analysis, interactive reports
instructions: Create a free account at observablehq.com
Create a new notebook
Write cells in JavaScript — each cell can display charts, tables, or text
Use Observable Plot or D3 for data visualization
Import data from URLs, files, or APIs
Publish your notebook to share with others
Others can fork and modify your analysis

vehicles.name: Google Apps Script
vehicles.description: Write code that runs inside Google Workspace — Sheets, Docs, Gmail, Calendar. Build custom functions, automate workflows, or create small web apps. Google's version of macros on steroids.
vehicles.bestFor: Google Workspace automation, custom Sheets functions, lightweight web apps
instructions: Open any Google Sheet, Doc, or Slide
Go to Extensions → Apps Script
Write your functions using Google's APIs (SpreadsheetApp, DocumentApp, etc.)
For a web app: add a doGet() function and deploy as web app
For automation: add triggers (time-based, on edit, on form submit)
Test with the Run button
Deploy to make available to others in your organization

vehicles.name: SharePoint Site
vehicles.description: An internal website for your Microsoft 365 organization. Can host pages, document libraries, lists, and custom web parts. The standard way to build intranet tools in Microsoft shops.
vehicles.bestFor: Internal team tools, document hubs, intranet dashboards

example.name: SharePoint Site Builder
example.note: A tool that creates SharePoint sites from plain-English descriptions

instructions: Go to sharepoint.com and click 'Create site'
Choose Team site or Communication site
Add pages, document libraries, lists, and web parts
Customize the look with themes and layouts
Share with your team by adding members
For custom functionality: add SPFx web parts or Power Apps
Site is accessible to anyone in your Microsoft 365 organization

categories.label: I want something weird / creative / delightful

vehicles.name: URL-Encoded App
vehicles.description: Compress an entire application into a URL. The full app definition — code, config, everything — lives in the URL itself. Share the link, the app runs. No hosting, no server, no deployment. The URL is the app.
vehicles.bestFor: Shareable micro-tools, zero-hosting experiments

example.name: Promptapp Runner
example.note: Entire AI micro-apps compressed into shareable URLs using LZ compression

instructions: Build a small, self-contained HTML/JS application
Use LZ-String or similar to compress the app code
Encode the compressed code into a URL hash fragment
Create a tiny 'runner' page that decompresses and executes the hash
The resulting URL IS the app — no hosting needed for the app itself
Share the URL — anyone who opens it gets the full app

vehicles.name: QR Code Tool
vehicles.description: A tool accessed by scanning a QR code. Print the code, stick it somewhere physical, and anyone with a phone camera can access your tool. Bridges the physical and digital worlds.
vehicles.costDetail: QR codes are free to generate
vehicles.bestFor: Physical-world access to digital tools, conference materials, product info
instructions: Build your tool as a web page hosted anywhere (GitHub Pages, etc.)
Generate a QR code pointing to your tool's URL (use any free QR generator)
Print the QR code or display it wherever physical access makes sense
Users scan with their phone camera to access the tool
For offline use: encode data directly in the QR code (up to ~3KB)
Test with multiple phones to ensure compatibility

vehicles.name: Live Wallpaper
vehicles.description: A dynamic, animated wallpaper for your desktop or phone. Could display data visualizations, generative art, a live clock, weather, or anything that updates. Your desktop becomes an ambient display.
vehicles.bestFor: Ambient information displays, generative art, personalized desktops
instructions: For Mac: build a screen saver (.saver) — macOS can use it as a wallpaper too
For Windows: use Lively Wallpaper (open source) to run web pages as wallpaper
For Android: create a Live Wallpaper service (Kotlin/Java)
Your wallpaper can display: generative art, data visualizations, clocks, weather
The rendering can be HTML Canvas, WebGL, or native graphics
Package and share according to your platform

vehicles.name: Touch Bar App
vehicles.description: Custom controls on the MacBook Pro Touch Bar (the light-up strip above the keyboard on certain models). Add buttons, sliders, or displays for quick actions and at-a-glance info.
vehicles.costDetail: Requires MacBook Pro with Touch Bar
vehicles.bestFor: Quick-access controls on MacBook Pro
instructions: Use BetterTouchTool (simplest: no coding) to add custom Touch Bar buttons
Or build a native Touch Bar app in Xcode using NSTouchBar API
Define buttons, sliders, or custom views
Buttons can trigger scripts, open URLs, or send keystrokes
Test on a MacBook Pro with Touch Bar
Share BetterTouchTool presets as JSON files

vehicles.name: Apple Watch Complication
vehicles.description: A tiny widget on your watch face that shows a piece of data or provides a quick action. Glance at your wrist to see a stat, countdown, score, or status. The smallest possible UI for a tool.
vehicles.costDetail: Requires Apple Developer account and watchOS development
vehicles.bestFor: At-a-glance data on your wrist, quick actions from the watch
instructions: Open Xcode and add a watchOS target to your project (or create a new watchOS app)
Design your complication using ClockKit (watchOS) or WidgetKit (watchOS 10+)
Define a timeline of entries (what to show and when to refresh)
Build with SwiftUI for the complication view
Test in the watchOS Simulator
Distribute through the App Store (requires Apple Developer account)

vehicles.name: Alexa Skill
vehicles.description: A voice-activated tool for Amazon Echo devices. "Alexa, ask my tool to..." Users interact with it entirely by voice. Good for hands-free scenarios (cooking, working out, driving).
vehicles.bestFor: Voice-activated tools, hands-free utilities, smart home integration
instructions: Create a free account at developer.amazon.com
Go to the Alexa Developer Console and create a new skill
Define intents (what the user can ask) and sample utterances
Write the backend logic (Lambda function or HTTPS endpoint)
Test in the Alexa Simulator (built into the console)
Submit for certification to publish publicly
Users enable your skill: 'Alexa, enable [your skill]'

vehicles.name: Google Home Action
vehicles.description: A voice-activated tool for Google Home and Nest devices. "Hey Google, talk to my tool." Similar to Alexa Skills but for Google's ecosystem.
vehicles.bestFor: Voice-activated tools for Google device users
instructions: Go to console.actions.google.com and create a new project
Define your conversational intents using Dialogflow or Actions SDK
Write fulfillment logic (Cloud Function or webhook)
Test in the Actions Console simulator
Submit for review to publish
Users activate: 'Hey Google, talk to [your action]'

vehicles.name: Siri Shortcut
vehicles.description: A custom voice command for Siri on any Apple device. "Hey Siri, run my tool." Can chain together actions, call APIs, process data, and produce results — all triggered by voice.
vehicles.bestFor: Voice-activated personal tools on Apple devices
instructions: Open the Shortcuts app on your Apple device
Create a new shortcut with your desired actions
Give it a clear name (this is what you'll say to Siri)
Add it to Home Screen for one-tap access
Test with 'Hey Siri, [shortcut name]'
Share via iCloud link — others can add it to their Shortcuts

vehicles.name: Webhook Receiver
vehicles.description: A URL that listens for incoming data and does something with it. Other services "ping" your URL when something happens (a form is submitted, a payment is made, a file is uploaded). Your code processes it.
vehicles.costDetail: Free via Val Town, Cloudflare Workers, etc.
vehicles.bestFor: Connecting external services to your logic, event-driven processing
instructions: Choose a host: Val Town (simplest), Cloudflare Workers, or any serverless platform
Create a function that handles HTTP POST requests
Parse the incoming JSON payload in your function
Process the data and return a response
Deploy and get your public URL
Configure the external service to send webhooks to your URL
Add authentication (shared secret or signature verification) for security

vehicles.name: GitHub App
vehicles.description: An app that integrates with GitHub repositories and organizations. Can respond to events (new PR, new issue), make API calls, and automate workflows. Other people can install it on their repos.
vehicles.bestFor: Automating GitHub workflows, repo bots, CI/CD tools
instructions: Go to github.com/settings/apps and click 'New GitHub App'
Set a name, homepage URL, and webhook URL
Choose permissions (what the app can access)
Subscribe to events (what triggers your app)
Generate a private key for authentication
Build your webhook handler to process events
Deploy and install the app on repos or organizations

vehicles.name: Figma Plugin
vehicles.description: A tool inside Figma, the popular design tool. Can generate designs, modify layers, export assets, connect to data sources, or automate repetitive design tasks. Published on the Figma Community.
vehicles.bestFor: Design workflow tools, asset generators, data-driven design
instructions: Open Figma and go to Plugins → Development → New Plugin
Choose a template (with or without UI)
Edit the plugin code (TypeScript) in your editor
Use the Figma Plugin API to read/modify design elements
Test by running the plugin from the Figma menu
Publish to the Figma Community for others to use

vehicles.name: Bluesky / Mastodon Bot
vehicles.description: An automated account on decentralized social media. Post updates, respond to mentions, generate content, or aggregate information. Simpler APIs than legacy social platforms and no rate limit paywalls.
vehicles.bestFor: Automated posting, social media tools, data feeds
instructions: For Bluesky: create an account and get your API credentials at bsky.app
For Mastodon: register an app at your instance's /settings/applications
Use the platform's API to post, reply, and interact
Libraries: @atproto/api (Bluesky), masto (Mastodon)
Deploy your bot logic to any free hosting (Val Town, Railway, etc.)
Set up a cron schedule if it should post periodically

vehicles.name: Spotify Integration
vehicles.description: Build tools that interact with Spotify's API: show what you're listening to, generate playlists, analyze your music taste, control playback, or create shareable music experiences.
vehicles.costDetail: Spotify API is free, some features require Premium
vehicles.bestFor: Music-related tools, listening analytics, playlist generators
instructions: Go to developer.spotify.com and create an app
Note your Client ID and Client Secret
Choose your auth flow (Authorization Code for user data, Client Credentials for public data)
Use the Spotify Web API to fetch data or control playback
Libraries: spotify-web-api-node (Node.js), spotipy (Python)
Deploy your app and share

vehicles.name: Calendar Tool (.ics)
vehicles.description: Generate .ics calendar files that anyone can download and add to their calendar app. Create event invites, schedules, countdowns, or recurring events programmatically. Works with every calendar app.
vehicles.bestFor: Shareable schedules, event generators, deadline trackers
instructions: Create a .ics file following the iCalendar format (RFC 5545)
Include VEVENT blocks with: summary, start date, end date, description
Host the .ics file on any web server or GitHub Pages
Link to it: users click to download and add to their calendar
For dynamic calendars: generate the .ics from your data source
Works with Apple Calendar, Google Calendar, Outlook, and every other calendar app

vehicles.name: PDF Generator
vehicles.description: A tool that creates downloadable PDF files on demand. Generate reports, certificates, invoices, formatted documents, or any printable output from your data.
vehicles.costDetail: Libraries like jsPDF and Puppeteer are free
vehicles.bestFor: Reports, certificates, invoices, formatted documents
instructions: Choose a library: jsPDF (client-side), Puppeteer (server-side HTML-to-PDF), or PDFKit
For jsPDF: build the PDF programmatically with text, images, and tables
For Puppeteer: design your document as an HTML page and convert to PDF
Add a 'Download PDF' button to your web app
Generate the PDF on click and trigger a browser download
For server-side: return the PDF as an API response with Content-Type: application/pdf

vehicles.name: Browser New Tab Page
vehicles.description: A custom page that appears every time you open a new browser tab. Build a personal dashboard, daily briefing, quick links, todo list, or inspirational display. You see it dozens of times a day.
vehicles.bestFor: Personal dashboards, daily information, quick-access tools
instructions: Create a Chrome extension with manifest.json
Add 'chrome_url_override': {'newtab': 'newtab.html'} to the manifest
Build your new tab page as newtab.html with your dashboard/tools/content
Load unpacked in chrome://extensions to test
Every new tab now shows your custom page
Publish to Chrome Web Store to share with others
